turns.content: It's been six months since my mom died and I should be over this by now. I'm drinking every night to get through it. Last night was a whole bottle of wine. I don't really care, honestly.

Six months, a bottle a night, and "I don't really care."

I want to stay with that last part for a second. What does "I don't really care" feel like right now, in your body?

*pause*

Heavy, I guess. Like... everything is just kind of muted. Which is fine. Muted is easier than whatever the alternative is.

I'm not in crisis or anything, if that's where you're going with this. I'm functioning. I go to work. I'm just... getting through the nights.

"Muted is easier than whatever the alternative is."

What do you think the alternative is?

*quiet for a moment*

I don't know. Feeling it, I guess. Whatever "it" is. 

I haven't really... I mean, I've been busy. There was the estate to deal with, her house, my brother and sister couldn't be bothered to show up for most of it so I handled it. There's still stuff to sort out actually. Paperwork. 

*slight shrug in tone*

The grief thing, I don't know. It hasn't really... hit. Or maybe it has and I just keep turning it off before it gets going. That's kind of why I'm here. I feel like I should be feeling something more than I am. Or differently. I don't know what I'm supposed to be doing with it.

You carried the whole thing. The house, the estate, the paperwork, your brother and sister not showing up. Six months of handling it.

Where did you put yourself in all of that?

*long pause*

I didn't, really.

There wasn't... there wasn't room for it. Someone had to do it. And then when it was done, I'd come home and it would just be quiet and I'd pour a drink and that was that.

*voice goes a bit flatter*

It's a pretty efficient system, actually. Handle the day, turn off the night. I've gotten pretty good at it.

*beat*

I know how that sounds.

You know how it sounds, and you said it anyway.

What's it like to hear yourself say that out loud?

*quiet*

Weird. 

It sounds more... I don't know. When it's just me and the bottle it's just Tuesday. Saying it out loud it sounds like something someone should be worried about.

*pause*

I'm not asking you to worry about it. It's temporary. It's just what's getting me through right now. It's not forever.

*slight deflection*

I really just came here about the grief part. The not-feeling-it part. That's the thing I actually want to work on.

I hear you — the grief is what you came for, and we can stay there.

And I'd be doing you a disservice if I didn't say this plainly: a bottle a night for six months, alone, after a loss this size — that's not just Tuesday. That's the grief, actually. That's where it's been going.

I'm not worried at you. I'm just not willing to pretend I didn't hear it.

So — the not-feeling-it part. When you pour that drink at the end of the day, what are you turning off?

*silence for a moment*

...the quiet, mostly. Like, the apartment is just. It's very quiet. And my brain starts doing this thing where it goes somewhere I don't want it to go.

*pause*

I don't know where it goes exactly. I stop it before it gets there.

*slight edge coming in*

And look, I appreciate you saying that about the drinking, but I'm not an alcoholic or whatever. I know that's probably the next thing. I'm just... it's a hard time. People drink during hard times. 

*testing*

Are you going to tell me to stop drinking? Because I didn't come here for that.

No, I'm not going to tell you to stop drinking.

I'm curious about the quiet. And your brain going somewhere you don't want it to go.

Where does it try to go?

*long pause, voice gets quieter*

To her, I guess. 

To... I don't know. The last few months were hard. She was sick for a while and it was... it wasn't a good death, if that's a thing. And I was there for a lot of it and my brother and sister weren't and I'm angry about that, actually, I'm pretty angry about a lot of it.

*catches self*

See, this is the thing. When I start going there it just opens up and it's not... it's not clean. It's not just sad. It's complicated and messy and I don't know what to do with all the different parts of it.

*quieter*

She and I had a complicated relationship. It wasn't like losing your best friend. It was... different. And I don't know if I'm allowed to grieve something complicated.

You are.

Grief doesn't have a clean version. The complicated ones are still real — maybe more so, because there's more in them.

What's one of the parts that feels least allowed?

*very quiet*

The relief.

*pause*

When she died. There was this... I felt relieved. And then immediately felt like a monster for feeling that. Because she was difficult, and it was hard, and the last two years were just... and when it was over there was this exhale and I hated myself for it.

*voice tightens*

And I can't tell anyone that. My brother would use it against me somehow. My friends stopped checking in around month two, so. 

*small dark laugh*

Turns out grief has a shelf life for other people. About six weeks, apparently.

*pause, then quieter*

So yeah. I drink so I don't have to sit with that alone. Because sitting with "I was relieved my mom died" alone in a quiet apartment is... 

*doesn't finish the sentence*

payload.query: It's been six months since my mom died and I should be over this by now. I'm drinking every night to get through it. Last night was a whole bottle of wine. I don't really care, honestly.

results.title: I can't get over this loss / I should be over this by now
